insights: Finance's significantly faster goal completion (mean 107.4 days) is driven almost entirely by category composition—50.7% of its goals are Cost Reduction, which complete in 22–36 days across all departments, versus only 16–21% in other departments.

Within every goal category—including Cost Reduction—Finance shows no consistent speed advantage over other departments, with only 2 out of 14 within-category comparisons statistically significant and zero advantage in its dominant Cost Reduction category.

Finance ranks 1st in completion speed but only 2nd in composite performance (49.47), trailing HR (52.08), confirming that its speed translates to above-average—but not top-tier—overall departmental performance.

Finance’s swift completion does not compromise goal quality or breadth: it shows no significant differences from other departments in state distribution (p=0.545), percent_complete patterns, or target achievement correlation (all r < 0.2, p > 0.17).

HR achieves the highest composite performance (52.08) despite slowest average completion time (156.8 days), indicating that sustained execution quality, not speed alone, drives top-tier departmental outcomes.

Cost Reduction goals are universally the fastest-completing category (22–36 days mean), yet their high frequency in Finance masks underlying parity in departmental execution capability—suggesting strategic goal portfolio design, not operational superiority, explains the speed differential.
summary: Finance completes goals significantly faster than other departments—not due to superior execution efficiency, but because over half (50.7%) of its goals fall into the inherently fast-completing Cost Reduction category, which averages just 22–36 days across all departments. Within-category analysis confirms Finance holds no speed advantage in any goal type, including Cost Reduction itself, definitively isolating category composition as the sole primary driver. While this speed lifts Finance to 1st in completion time and 2nd in composite performance (49.47), HR outperforms it overall (52.08) despite slower completion—demonstrating that balanced goal achievement, not velocity alone, determines top-tier departmental performance. Critically, Finance’s speed does not come at the cost of goal quality, coverage, or target alignment, as evidenced by non-significant differences in state distribution, percent_complete, and target achievement correlations. The key strategic implication is that departmental performance comparisons must be normalized for goal category mix to avoid misattributing portfolio effects to operational capability—future resource and governance decisions should prioritize category-aware benchmarking and intentional goal allocation.
